raw.reasoning: The most likely authorities are the Federal Supreme Court decisions on the commencement of the relative limitation period for bodily injury claims under Art. 60 OR, applied by analogy to Art. 83 SVG, and the restrictive exoneration standard under Art. 59 Abs. 1 SVG. On prescription, the key line of cases holds that the period does not begin merely when the claimant suspects lasting consequences, but only when the damage is sufficiently stabilized and can be estimated in its essential elements, especially future loss of earnings. In a fact pattern involving head injury, neuropsychological sequelae and later AI/IV recognition of a 45% earning incapacity, courts typically treat the social-insurance decision and/or a sufficiently reliable expert assessment as the earliest point at which future economic loss became adequately knowable. That tends to support the view that claim enlargements in 2015 and 2016 were not prescribed if the decisive knowledge arose only with the November 2014 disability decision or shortly before. On exoneration, the leading SVG cases stress that gross negligence of the injured party is interpreted narrowly; even a traffic-rule violation such as riding counter-flow does not automatically exonerate the holder unless it constitutes a particularly serious fault that is the sole or overwhelmingly predominant cause and the accident is not also attributable to the operational risk or driving conduct of the motor vehicle. In junction-exit situations, courts are generally reluctant to admit full exoneration and more often consider reduction for contributory fault rather than complete release under Art. 59 Abs. 1 SVG.

raw.reasoning: The most likely authorities cluster around three issues. First, Art. 115 OR: Swiss law treats debt remission as a contract, so the key precedents are those stating that a remission requires concordant wills and that silence normally does not equal acceptance; cases on interpretation of settlement letters under the Vertrauensprinzip are therefore central. Second, KKG/LCC applicability: the decisive authorities are those limiting the KKG to consumer transactions and excluding financing/leasing for professional or commercial purposes under Arts. 1, 7 and 8 KKG. Third, refurbishment costs after return of the vehicle: the likely cited precedents are lease-law decisions on Arts. 267 and 267a OR concerning ordinary wear, excessive deterioration, immediate inspection and notice, plus decisions on whether those rules apply directly or only subsidiarily/analogically to vehicle leasing or business hire contracts. If KKG is excluded because the truck was expressly for business operations, the dispute will likely be resolved under the contract, general OR rules (especially Arts. 97 and 143 OR), and possibly by analogy to Arts. 267/267a OR. The listed cases are the authorities most likely to be invoked for those propositions.

raw.reasoning: The most likely decisive authority is BGE 137 III 226, which is the principal Federal Supreme Court precedent on the PrHG and addresses the notion of defect under Art. 4 PrHG, the claimant’s burden to prove defect/damage/causation, and the producer’s exoneration under Art. 5 PrHG. In a fact pattern involving premature wear, missing preservation of the removed component, photographs, an expert report based on incomplete material, and technical literature about similar failures, Swiss courts typically focus first on whether the claimant has positively established that the product failed to provide the safety one may legitimately expect, not merely that it wore out earlier than hoped or than comparable products. If defect is not proven, the claim fails already under Art. 4 PrHG and there is no need to rely on Art. 5 PrHG. If the court nevertheless reaches exoneration, the most relevant precedents are those discussing the producer’s proof that the defect did not exist when the product was put into circulation (Art. 5 lit. b PrHG) or that the defect could not be discovered according to the state of scientific and technical knowledge at that time (Art. 5 lit. e PrHG). Because PrHG case law is comparatively sparse, courts also cite general civil-law precedents on burden of proof, proof by indicia, expert evidence, and evidentiary appreciation under OR/ZPO/BGG. Those are included here because they are commonly used to resolve exactly the evidentiary issues raised by this scenario.

raw.reasoning: The most likely authorities are the classic physician-liability and mandate-law decisions. For issue (1), Swiss case law traditionally qualifies the Arztvertrag as an Auftrag under Art. 394 ff. OR, because the physician generally owes conscientious treatment according to professional standards, not a guaranteed therapeutic success; BGE 110 II 375 and BGE 106 II 157 are the classic citations. For issue (2), the central precedents are BGE 120 II 248 and BGE 117 II 563, which define the physician’s duty of care under Art. 398 OR by reference to the rules of medical science, specialist standards, and expert evidence; these are the authorities most likely to be cited for omissions such as failure to perform indicated preoperative diagnostics or incomplete follow-up procedures. For issue (3), the likely citations are the mandate-law cases on remuneration, accounting and termination—especially BGE 124 III 423 (Art. 404 OR), BGE 115 II 464 (fees despite defective or useless performance), and BGE 122 III 61 / BGE 127 III 328 (Art. 400 OR and mandatary duties). Together these authorities support the propositions that negligent treatment can trigger contractual liability for the costs of corrective treatment by another specialist, that the patient may terminate the mandate when trust is lost, and that the physician’s fee claim may be reduced or excluded, with unjustified advances having to be refunded, where the services were defective, incomplete, or of no benefit.

Mangels einer einzigen alles entscheidenden Leitentscheidung zur exakt gleichen Konstellation ist eine Kombination aus GAV-Rechtsprechung, AVG/AVV-Vollzugspraxis und Lohnqualifikationsentscheiden am ehesten massgeblich.

The most likely authorities are therefore the leading BGE on nachehelicher Unterhalt and on mandate/simple partnership/accounting, plus more recent 5A and 4A decisions applying those principles. On these facts, Swiss courts are more likely to characterize the arrangement as a fiduciary mandate rather than a simple partnership if the wife merely entrusted money to the husband to invest in her name and there was no genuine joint purpose or shared organizational structure. In that event, the husband’s duty to account and restitute under Art. 400 OR becomes central, and his failure to keep records strongly supports restitution of unaccounted funds. By contrast, if a true simple partnership were found, liquidation/accounting would proceed under Arts. 533 and 537 OR. For maintenance, the cited Art. 125 ZGB cases are the standard authorities for denying or limiting support where the marriage occurred at advanced age, lasted only a limited period, and did not fundamentally reshape the claimant’s economic life in a legally relevant way.

Zweitens ist für Morgan die Rechtsprechung zum hypothetischen Einkommen zentral: Ein solches darf erst angerechnet werden, wenn die Erwerbstätigkeit zumutbar und tatsächlich möglich ist; nach Ende des Bezugs von Arbeitslosenleistungen braucht es konkrete Feststellungen zu Alter, bisheriger Tätigkeit im Familienbetrieb, Arbeitsmarkt und Übergangsfrist. Second, it addresses the absence of a remedies notice and filing deadline, linking this to the constitutional protection of good faith; a late challenge may be admitted if the addressee could rely on the omission and the error was not recognizable with ordinary diligence. ZPO, including business/trade secrecy. raw.reasoning: The fact pattern strongly points to Federal Supreme Court case 5A_214/2016 as the most directly relevant authority. The legal issues are twofold: first, whether Swiss courts retain jurisdiction to order and maintain urgent/protective spousal maintenance under Art. 10 IPRG and the matrimonial-protection framework (Art. 172 ff., especially Art. 176 ZGB; procedurally now Art. 271 ff. and 276 ZPO) while divorce proceedings are pending abroad in the spouses’ common national state under Art. 59/62/63 IPRG; second, whether a Moroccan divorce judgment/instrument can be recognised under Art. 65 IPRG despite the parties not appearing personally, where the respondent was represented by counsel. On the first issue, the likely controlling line of authority holds that Swiss courts may grant urgent provisional measures on the basis of Art. 10 IPRG when there is a sufficient Swiss connection and a need for immediate protection, even if the merits are pending abroad; such measures can subsist until the foreign decision capable of recognition takes effect in Switzerland. On the second issue, the leading recognition cases stress that Swiss ordre public is applied restrictively: lack of personal appearance alone does not bar recognition if the party was duly summoned, had an opportunity to be heard, and was effectively represented by counsel. A foreign divorce from the common national state of the spouses is in principle recognisable under Art. 65 IPRG unless there is a serious substantive or procedural incompatibility with Swiss public policy. The listed BGE decisions are the most likely general authorities, and 5A_214/2016 is the most likely directly on-point individual case.

raw.reasoning: The most likely authorities split into two groups. First are family-law precedents on Art. 2 Abs. 2 ZGB, Art. 159/163/176 ZGB, provisio ad litem, and the summary nature of Eheschutz proceedings. These cases are typically cited to determine whether a formally existing marriage can still ground maintenance, how maintenance is assessed when there was no real marital life, and what evidentiary threshold applies in protective measures. Second are immigration-law sham-marriage cases under Art. 51, 62 and 63 AIG, which are often used by analogy for the factual indicia of a Scheinehe: no common household, no shared finances, instrumental preservation of the marriage for permit purposes, and lack of genuine marital community. In a case framed exactly as here, the strongest citation is likely the specific family-law decision 5A_777/2014, together with the classic abuse-of-rights authority BGE 121 III 149 and the standard Art. 176 ZGB maintenance cases. If abuse is not accepted, the court would then fall back on the ordinary Eheschutz maintenance framework from the BGE line on actual marital standard of living, need, earning capacity, and provisio ad litem.
